back: A subgraph is a graph formed from a subset of the vertices and a subset of the edges of a given graph G. If H is a subgraph of G, then V(H) ⊆ V(G) and E(H) ⊆ E(G).
A spanning subgraph is a subgraph H of a graph G that contains all the vertices of G (V(H) = V(G)) but not necessarily all of the edges (E(H) ⊆ E(G)).
An induced subgraph is a subgraph H of G that is formed by taking a subset S of the vertices of G and all of the edges in G that have both endpoints in S. It is denoted as G[S].

front: What is the key difference between a general subgraph and an induced subgraph?
back: A general subgraph can have any subset of edges from G connecting its chosen vertices. An induced subgraph must include every edge from G that exists between its chosen vertices.

front: Can a single vertex be considered a subgraph?
back: Yes, a single vertex is a valid subgraph. It is a subgraph formed by a subset of the vertices (containing one vertex) and a subset of the edges (containing no edges).
